metadata.notes: Standalone Uprising game — not the original Dune: Imperium and not an expansion. Sources: official Dire Wolf rulebook, RulesPal, BGG strategy threads, Reddit, Dire Wolf design diaries.

subtopics.title: Components
subtopics.content:
#### Boards and Tracks
- **1 main game board** (two-sided: standard for 1-4 players, expanded for 5-6 players) — Depicts Arrakis with worker-placement spaces organized by faction (Emperor, Spacing Guild, Bene Gesserit, Fremen) plus neutral locations.
- **4 faction influence tracks** along the board edges — Emperor, Spacing Guild, Bene Gesserit, Fremen.
- **Conflict area** — Central zone where combat is resolved each round.
- **Shield Wall token** — Placed below Spice Refinery; blocks sandworm summoning at three board locations until destroyed.

#### Cards
- **Starting deck** — 10 cards per player (identical): 2 Convincing Argument, 2 Dagger, 1 Diplomacy, 2 Dune the Desert Planet, 1 Reconnaissance, 1 Seek Allies, 1 Signet Ring.
- **Imperium deck** (69 cards) — Large market deck of cards to acquire.
- **Reserve cards** — Always available: Prepare the Way and The Spice Must Flow.
- **Intrigue cards** (44 cards) — Secret cards: Plot, Combat, or Endgame types.
- **Conflict cards** (16 cards) — Separated into tiers I, II, and III. One revealed per round.

#### Per Player
- **3 Agents** — 2 start on your Leader card, 1 (Swordmaster) set aside to unlock.
- **16 cubes** — 4 faction influence markers + 12 troops.
- **2 discs** — Score marker + Combat marker.
- **3 Spy cylinders** — NEW in Uprising. Placed on observation posts around the board.
- **3 Control markers** — Placed when you win control of key locations.

#### Resources
- **Spice tokens** (27) — Primary valuable resource, harvested from desert locations.
- **Solari tokens** (28) — Money, used to pay for board space costs and purchases.
- **Water tokens** (20) — Required for certain Arrakis actions and sandworm summoning.

#### Uprising-Specific Components
- **8 Sandworm tokens** — Powerful combat units, 3 strength each.
- **4 Maker Hook tokens** — Permanent upgrades that let you summon sandworms.
- **4 Alliance tokens** — One per faction, awarded to first player reaching 4 influence.
- **9 Leader cards** (two-sided) — Asymmetric player powers.
- **Objective cards** — Determine first player and provide battle icon scoring opportunities.

subtopics.title: Board Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Board Setup
1. **Place the game board** — Use the standard side for 1-4 players. Flip to the expanded side for 5-6.
2. **Shield Wall** — Place the Shield Wall token below the Spice Refinery space.
3. **Alliance tokens** — Place one Alliance token on each of the four faction tracks at the level 4 space.
4. **Conflict deck** — Separate Conflict cards by tier. Shuffle each tier. Stack: 4 Conflict III on bottom, 5 Conflict II in the middle, 1 Conflict I on top.
5. **Imperium market** — Shuffle the Imperium deck. Deal 5 cards face up as the market row.
6. **Reserve cards** — Place Prepare the Way and The Spice Must Flow stacks beside the market.
7. **Intrigue deck** — Shuffle and place face down.
8. **Resource supply** — Place all spice, Solari, and water tokens in the general supply.

#### Player Setup
1. **Choose a Leader** — Each player selects a unique Leader card (no duplicates from the same House). Each Leader is two-sided with different abilities.
2. **Take starting deck** — 10 cards. Shuffle and draw 5 as your opening hand.
3. **Place Agents** — Put 2 Agents on your Leader card. Set the Swordmaster aside (unlockable later).
4. **Take resources** — 1 water token.
5. **Troops** — Place troops in your garrison area.
6. **Markers** — Place score marker at 0 on the VP track, combat marker at 0.
7. **Faction markers** — Place 1 cube at 0 on each of the 4 influence tracks.
8. **Spies** — Take 3 Spy cylinders in your color.
9. **Control markers** — Take 3 Control markers.

#### First Player
Deal 1 Objective card to each player. The player whose Objective shows the first-player icon goes first. Keep Objectives face up — their battle icons matter for scoring throughout the game.

subtopics.title: Game Overview
subtopics.content:
#### Core Concept
Dune: Imperium Uprising is a hybrid deck-building and worker-placement game. Each card in your hand serves a dual purpose: play it on your Agent turn to send a worker to a matching board space, or save it for your Reveal turn where it contributes combat strength and persuasion. This dual-use tension is the heart of every decision.

#### Victory Condition
The game ends when any player reaches 10 or more Victory Points at the end of a round, or when the Conflict deck is exhausted. Highest VP wins. Tiebreakers: most spice, then Solari, then water, then garrisoned troops.

#### Three Paths to Victory Points
- **Winning Conflicts** — Conflict cards award VP directly. Sandworm rewards can be doubled.
- **Faction Influence** — Reaching level 2 on a faction track earns 1 VP. Reaching level 4 and claiming an Alliance token earns another 1 VP.
- **The Spice Must Flow** — A reserve card costing 9 persuasion that grants 1 VP when acquired.

#### Game Arc
- **Conflict I** (Round 1) — Low stakes, players establish board position.
- **Conflict II** (Rounds 2-6) — Escalating rewards, faction races heat up.
- **Conflict III** (Rounds 7-10) — High VP conflicts decide the winner.

subtopics.title: Round Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Five Phases Per Round

**Phase 1: Round Start**
Reveal the top card of the Conflict deck — this shows what everyone is fighting over this round. Each player draws 5 cards from their deck.

**Phase 2: Player Turns**
Starting with the first player and going clockwise, each player takes one turn at a time. On your turn, you must choose one of two options:

**Option A — Agent Turn:**
1. Play 1 card from your hand. Look at the Agent icons on the card — these tell you which board spaces you can visit.
2. Send 1 Agent from your Leader card to a matching, unoccupied board space. Pay any costs the space requires.
3. Resolve the card's Agent box effect (ignore the Reveal box for now).
4. Gain the board space's effects.
5. If you sent your Agent to a Combat space: deploy troops from your garrison to the Conflict (up to 2, plus any recruited this turn), then advance your Combat marker.

**Option B — Reveal Turn:**
1. Reveal all remaining cards in your hand face up.
2. Resolve all Reveal box effects (combat strength, persuasion, etc.).
3. Set your total Combat strength: troops contribute 2 each, sandworms 3 each, sword icons from revealed cards 1 each.
4. Spend Persuasion to buy cards from the 5-card Imperium Row or Reserve. Purchased cards go to your discard pile.
5. Clean up — all played and revealed cards go to your discard pile.

Once you take a Reveal turn, you are done for this round. You must have at least 1 unit in the Conflict to contribute any combat strength. Unspent Persuasion is lost.

**Phase 3: Combat**
Starting with the first player, each player with units in the Conflict may play Combat Intrigue cards or pass. Continue around until all players pass consecutively.

Resolve combat by ranking total strength:
- 1st place wins the Conflict card and its top reward. Check for battle icon matches.
- 2nd place gets the secondary reward.
- 3rd place gets the tertiary reward (in 4-6 player games).
- Ties for 1st: all tied players get 2nd-place rewards; no one takes the Conflict card.

After combat: troops return to supply (not garrison). Combat markers reset to 0. Sandworms return to the bank.

**Phase 4: Makers**
Check Deep Desert, Hagga Basin, and Imperial Basin (plus Habbanya Erg in 6-player). If a space has no Agent on it, add 1 bonus spice from the bank. This spice accumulates across rounds.

**Phase 5: Recall**
Check the endgame trigger (10 VP). If not triggered: recall all Agents to Leader cards, pass the first player marker clockwise, and start a new round.

subtopics.title: Factions and Influence
subtopics.content:
#### The Four Factions
Four political factions control Arrakis. Each has dedicated board spaces and an influence track.

**Emperor (House Corrino)** — Provides wealth (Solari) and Sardaukar elite troops. Sending an Agent to Emperor spaces advances your Emperor influence.

**Spacing Guild** — Controls interstellar travel. Access to the Heighliner (deploy 5 troops for 6 spice — a massive combat swing). Advancing Guild influence opens powerful logistics.

**Bene Gesserit** — Masters of manipulation. Provides card draw, intrigue cards, and subtle advantages. Rewards efficiency and hand optimization.

**Fremen** — Desert warriors native to Arrakis. Provides access to Sietch Tabr, Maker Hooks, and the power to destroy the Shield Wall. Strong synergy with sandworms.

#### How Influence Works
- Sending an Agent to any faction-aligned board space automatically grants +1 influence with that faction.
- **Level 2** — Earn 1 VP (lost if you drop below 2).
- **Level 4** — Earn a permanent track bonus. The first player to reach 4 claims that faction's Alliance token (+1 VP). If another player later surpasses you on the track, they steal the Alliance token.
- Some board spaces and cards require minimum influence levels to access (for example, Imperial Privilege requires 2 Emperor influence).

#### Strategic Note
Specialize in 1-2 factions rather than spreading thin across all four. Shallow investment yields no alliances and no VP.

subtopics.title: Spies
subtopics.content:
#### Spy Mechanic (New in Uprising)
Each player has 3 Spy cylinders. Spies are placed on observation posts — small circular spaces connected to board spaces by dotted lines.

#### Placing Spies
Certain card effects and board space rewards let you place a Spy on an unoccupied observation post of your choice. Once placed, a Spy stays there until you recall it.

#### Using Spies (Three Ways)

**1. Infiltrate** — When you want to send an Agent to a board space that is already occupied by an opponent's Agent, you can recall a Spy from a connected observation post to ignore the opponent's Agent and place yours there anyway. This is Uprising's answer to getting blocked.

**2. Gather Intelligence** — When you send an Agent to a board space connected to one of your Spies, you may recall that Spy to draw 1 card from your deck. A flexible hand-refill option.

**3. Spy Agent Icon** — Some cards show the Spy icon. When you play a card with this icon, you may send your Agent to any board space that is connected to one of your Spies, without recalling the Spy. The Spy acts as a universal key for that turn.

#### Spy Restrictions
- You cannot use the same Spy for both Infiltrate and Gather Intelligence on the same turn.
- Observation posts are limited — only one Spy per post.
- Recalling a Spy removes it from the board. You will need another effect to place it again.

subtopics.title: Sandworms and the Shield Wall
subtopics.content:
#### Sandworms (New in Uprising)
Sandworms are powerful one-time-use combat units. Each sandworm contributes 3 combat strength.

#### How to Get Sandworms
1. First, acquire a **Maker Hook** token. You can earn these at Sietch Tabr (requires 2 Fremen influence) and from certain card effects.
2. Once you have a Maker Hook, you can summon sandworms via specific board spaces (like Deep Desert — costs water) or card effects.
3. Sandworms go directly into the Conflict area. They cannot be garrisoned.

#### Reward Doubling
This is the signature Uprising mechanic: if you have at least 1 sandworm in the Conflict when rewards are distributed, all your rewards are doubled. This applies to VP, spice, Solari, troops, and other rewards — but not to Control placement or battle icons. A well-timed sandworm win can be worth 4 VP in a single round.

#### The Shield Wall
Three board locations — Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, and Imperial Basin — are protected by the Shield Wall. Sandworms cannot be summoned to Conflicts at these locations while the Shield Wall stands.

Players can permanently destroy the Shield Wall via the detonation icon at Sietch Tabr or certain Intrigue cards. Once destroyed, sandworms can enter all Conflicts for the rest of the game.

#### After Combat
Sandworms always return to the bank after combat. They are never kept between rounds.

subtopics.title: Deck Building and Intrigue
subtopics.content:
#### Deck Building
Your starting deck of 10 cards will grow as you acquire cards from the Imperium Row and Reserve.

- **Acquiring cards** — During your Reveal turn, spend Persuasion (from revealed card effects) to buy cards. Purchased cards go to your discard pile.
- **Deck cycling** — When your draw deck is empty, shuffle your discard pile to form a new deck.
- **Trashing** — Some effects let you permanently remove cards from your deck. This thins your deck so you draw your best cards more often. Trashing weak starting cards like Daggers is very powerful.

#### Card Dual Purpose
Every card has two boxes of text:
- **Agent box** — Used when you play the card on an Agent turn. It shows Agent icons (which board spaces you can visit) and Agent effects.
- **Reveal box** — Used when the card is revealed during your Reveal turn. Typically provides combat strength (sword icons) or Persuasion for buying cards.

The key tension: a card played as an Agent cannot contribute its Reveal effects, and vice versa.

#### Intrigue Cards
Intrigue cards are drawn from a separate deck and kept secret in your hand.
- **Plot** — Playable during your Agent or Reveal turn for immediate effects.
- **Combat** — Only playable during the Combat phase (Phase 3).
- **Endgame** — Played after the final round for VP. Keep these hidden until scoring.

#### Control
Winning Conflicts at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, or Imperial Basin places your Control marker on that location.
- Opponents must pay you 1 extra Solari or spice when they send Agents there.
- You get a defensive bonus (1 free troop deployment) when that location's Conflict reappears.

#### Battle Icons
Conflict cards show one of three battle icons: Crysknife, Desert Mouse, or Ornithopter. When you win a Conflict, check if the battle icon matches any face-up Conflict card you have already won. If so, flip the matched pair face down for 1 bonus VP.

subtopics.title: Scoring and Endgame
subtopics.content:
#### End-Game Trigger
The game ends at the end of the round in which any player reaches 10 or more VP, or when the Conflict deck is exhausted.

#### VP Sources (During Game)
- **Conflict wins** — VP from 1st-place Conflict card rewards.
- **Battle icon pairs** — 1 VP for each matched pair of Conflict cards.
- **Faction influence** — 1 VP at level 2 on any faction track (lost if you drop below).
- **Alliance tokens** — 1 VP each (from reaching level 4 first on a faction track).
- **The Spice Must Flow** — 1 VP each when acquired from the Reserve.
- **Certain Imperium and Intrigue cards** — Grant VP through various effects.

#### Final Scoring
After the final round, all players may play Endgame Intrigue cards for bonus VP. Total all VP from all sources. Highest VP wins.

#### Tiebreaker
Most spice, then most Solari, then most water, then most garrisoned troops.

subtopics.title: Beginner Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Three VP Paths
Most winning strategies focus on one primary path with a secondary:

**1. Combat Dominance** — Win Conflicts for direct VP. Pair with sandworm reward doubling for explosive turns. Needs troops in garrison and combat cards.

**2. Spice Must Flow Engine** — Build Persuasion to buy The Spice Must Flow repeatedly (1 VP each at 9 Persuasion). Needs a strong economic engine.

**3. Faction Alliances** — Rush influence tracks to level 2 (1 VP) and level 4 (Alliance token, another VP). Reliable but limited ceiling.

Pick one primary path and one secondary. Trying all three spreads you too thin.

#### Key Principles

**Deck Thinning Wins Games**
Your starting deck has weak cards. Every time you trash a Dagger or a 1-Persuasion card, your average draw quality goes up. Think of trashing as sharpening your deck into a precise tool. Prioritize trash effects early.

**Cards Are Dual-Purpose — Choose Wisely**
Every card played as an Agent is one fewer card contributing combat strength on your Reveal turn. If a big Conflict is coming, consider holding strong sword cards for Reveal instead of using them as Agents.

**Garrison Management**
Keep 2-4 troops in your garrison at all times. Phase III Conflict cards offer huge rewards, and showing up empty-handed is devastating. Recruit troops steadily, not all at once.

**Specialize in 1-2 Factions**
Pushing all four influence tracks equally is a losing strategy. Pick factions that align with your VP path. Fremen pairs with sandworms. Spacing Guild pairs with combat (Heighliner). Emperor pairs with economy.

#### Spy Placement Tips
- Place Spies on spaces you expect heavy competition for — Swordmaster, High Council, key faction spaces.
- Infiltrate is strongest when it lets you take a contested space an opponent thought was safe.
- Do not hoard Spies forever. Recalling to Gather Intelligence at the right moment (drawing a card when you need answers) is often more valuable than holding the position.

#### Sandworm Tips
- Getting a Maker Hook early is often more valuable than an early Swordmaster in Uprising.
- Sandworms cannot be garrisoned. They go straight to the Conflict and return to the bank after combat.
- The reward-doubling effect is transformative. Even a 2nd-place finish with a sandworm can yield more than 1st place without one.
- Remember the Shield Wall blocks sandworms at Arrakeen, Spice Refinery, and Imperial Basin until someone destroys it.

#### Combat Mindset
Think of combat as a blind auction. Do not overspend resources on low-value Conflict I cards. Save your big pushes for Phase II and III Conflicts where the rewards justify the investment. Second and third place still give consolation rewards — sometimes a partial commitment is the right play.

#### Common Beginner Mistakes
- **Buying too many mediocre cards** — Every card added dilutes your draws. Be selective.
- **Ignoring deck thinning** — Your starting Daggers actively hurt you in the midgame.
- **Spreading across all four factions** — Pick 1-2 and commit.
- **Chasing Swordmaster over Maker Hook** — In Uprising specifically, sandworm reward doubling often outweighs the third Agent.
- **Neglecting the garrison** — Phase III Conflicts are worth huge VP. Show up prepared.
- **Ignoring battle icons** — Matching icon pairs for bonus VP adds up fast. Factor icons into combat decisions.

subtopics.title: Advanced Strategy
subtopics.content:
#### Faction Synergies

**Fremen + Sandworms (Desert Power)**
The premier combo in Uprising. Push Fremen influence to access Sietch Tabr, earn Maker Hooks, and optionally destroy the Shield Wall. Once online, every Conflict win is doubled. Pair with Fremen Reveal effects for massive combat swings.

**Spacing Guild + Combat (Heighliner Surprise)**
The Heighliner deploys 5 troops for 6 spice — a game-changing combat swing. Best used late in a round after opponents have already committed their forces. The threat alone forces opponents to over-commit. Guild influence at level 2+ unlocks the Shipping space for additional spice flow.

**Emperor + Economy (Solari Engine)**
Emperor spaces provide Solari and Sardaukar troops. Build a Solari engine that funds expensive plays and The Spice Must Flow purchases. Works well as a flexible mid-game pivot.

**Bene Gesserit + Deck Efficiency**
Bene Gesserit rewards card manipulation and intrigue accumulation. Pairs naturally with deck-thinning strategies and Endgame Intrigue card hoarding. A strong choice for players who want to win through efficiency.

#### Advanced Combat Tactics

**Tempo Reads**
Plan your opening two turns carefully. In Rounds 1-2, you have near-perfect information about your draws. Map out your sequence: Turn 1 Stillsuits into Turn 2 Great Flat, or Hagga Basin into Sell Melange.

**Heighliner Timing**
Never telegraph a Heighliner play. The ideal sequence: opponents commit troops to a Conflict, you deploy 5 troops as a surprise on your last Agent turn before Reveal. The uncertainty of your spice reserves is your weapon.

**Sandworm Math**
With reward doubling, even 2nd place can be worth more than 1st without sandworms. Calculate whether your worm investment pays off before committing. A 2 VP Conflict doubled to 4 VP with one sandworm is almost always worth it.

**Bluffing**
Hidden Reveal effects (especially Fremen sword icons) create a bluffing dynamic. Deploying fewer troops while holding strong Reveal cards catches opponents off guard. Conversely, heavy troop deployment with weak Reveal cards can bluff opponents into conceding.

#### Leader Selection

**Strong Leaders (Community Consensus)**
- **Feyd-Rautha** — Built-in deck trashing. Automatically thins your deck every round, which is extremely powerful.
- **Lady Margot Fenring** — Spy placement synergy. Dominates board control through espionage.
- **Duke Leto** — Landsraad spaces cost 1 less Solari. Signet Ring ability lets you gain influence where opponents lead you. Consistently useful across all strategies.
- **Gurney Halleck** — Summons a free troop to garrison each round. Reliable combat readiness.

**General Leader Advice**
Pick a Leader whose ability aligns with your intended VP path. Leaders with built-in trashing or resource generation rank highest because they provide value every round without costing actions.

#### The CHOAM Module (Optional)
Adds contracts — small objectives that award resources or VP when completed. If playing with CHOAM: grab contracts early that align with board spaces you already plan to visit. Do not chase contracts that pull you off your main strategy. Immediate-completion contracts are the most efficient.

rulings: A player must have at least 1 unit (troop or sandworm) in the Conflict area to contribute any combat strength. Revealed sword icons alone do not count if you have zero units deployed.
Sandworm reward doubling applies to VP, spice, Solari, troops, water, and card draws from Conflict rewards. It does NOT double Control marker placement or battle icon collection.
You cannot use the same Spy for both Infiltrate and Gather Intelligence on the same Agent turn. Pick one benefit per Spy per turn.
Troops lost in combat return to supply, NOT to garrison. You must re-recruit them to garrison before they can be deployed again.
The Shield Wall destruction is permanent and irreversible. Once destroyed, sandworms can enter all Conflicts for the rest of the game.
When tied for 1st place in combat, all tied players receive 2nd-place rewards and no one takes the Conflict card. When tied for 2nd, all tied players receive 3rd-place rewards.
Sandworms cannot be garrisoned. When summoned, they go directly to the Conflict area. After combat, they always return to the bank regardless of whether you won or lost.
Alliance tokens are stolen when an opponent surpasses your influence level on that faction track. The VP moves with the token — you lose 1 VP and they gain 1 VP.
Endgame Intrigue cards are played AFTER the final round ends but before final scoring. They are not revealed during gameplay.
The Maker Hook token is a permanent upgrade. Once acquired, you keep it for the rest of the game. You do not spend it to summon sandworms — you need it plus water or card effects.
